original_abstract: This paper introduces Stanford CoreNLP, an integrated suite of natural language processing tools designed as an extensible Java pipeline. The toolkit provides a comprehensive range of linguistic analysis capabilities, including tokenization, part-of-speech tagging, named entity recognition, syntactic parsing, and coreference resolution. Historically developed to replace fragmented, ad hoc processing scripts with a unified API, the system focuses on a lightweight architecture that represents annotations as typesafe maps. We examine the design philosophy behind CoreNLP, attributing its widespread adoption in both academia and industry to its "minimal baggage" approach: it offers high-quality, robust components with a straightforward interface that avoids the steep learning curves associated with heavier frameworks like UIMA or GATE. The paper details the system’s primary annotators, its support for multiple human languages, and the ease with which users can implement and integrate custom analysis modules. By emphasizing simplicity, documentation, and ease of deployment, Stanford CoreNLP serves as a highly accessible yet powerful solution for diverse text analytics tasks.
humanized_abstract: We describe Stanford CoreNLP, a set of libraries providing an extensible Java annotation pipeline for processing natural language. This work provides an extensive set of annotations while keeping the architecture lightweight. Our primary annotators include word-level tokenization, sentence-level segmentation, pos tagging, named entity recognition, parsing, and coreference. The original goal of this project was to replace a proliferation of ad hoc scripts with a more comprehensive and consistent API for NLP processing. Today, the "minimal baggage" design of CoreNLP — providing high quality, general-purpose components via a simple and easy-to-use API — has enabled rapid development and widespread use of CoreNLP in both research and production environments, while keeping the system small enough to avoid the overhead associated with more comprehensive frameworks such as UIMA or GATE. We present a high-level overview of the system, discuss the core annotators, and describe the set of languages currently supported, in addition to discussing how easy it is to add new annotators. We conclude by highlighting the simplicity, ease of use, and ease of deployment of Stanford CoreNLP as a premier toolkit for text processing.
